title: Terence Tao – How the world’s top mathematician uses AI

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Okay today I'm starting with Terence Tao who needs no introduction. Terence I want to begin by having you retell the story of how Kepler discovered the laws of planetary motion because I think this will be a great jumping off point to talk about AI for math.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: Okay yeah so I've always had an amateur interest in astronomy and so I've I've loved stories of how the early astronomers worked out the nature of the universe.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: So uh Kepler was building on the work of Copernicus who was himself building on the work of Aristarchus. So uh Copernicus very famously proposed the heliocentric model that uh instead of the planets and sun going around the earth that the sun was at the center of the solar system and the other planets were were going around the sun. And Copernicus proposed that the orbits of the planets were perfect circles.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: And his theory kind of fit the observations that the the Greeks and the Arabs and Indians had worked out over over centuries. I think Kepler got interested he like he learned about these these theories in his in his studies and he made this observation that the ratios of the size of the orbits that Copernicus had predicted seemed to have some geometric meaning.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: I think he he started proposing that you know if you if you take say the orbit of say the earth and you enclose it in I think maybe a cube the outer sphere of that that encloses the cube almost match perfectly to the orbit of Mars and so forth. And there were six planets known at the time five gaps between them and there were five perfect Platonic solids.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: The cube the tetrahedron icosahedron octahedron and dodecahedron. And so he had this this theory which he thought was absolutely beautiful that he could inscribe these Platonic solids between the spheres of the planets and it seemed to fit and it it it seemed to him like you know God's design of the planets was was matching this mathematical perfection of the Platonic solids.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: So he needed data to confirm this theory and at the time there was only one really high quality data set almost in existence okay which was so Tycho Brahe this Danish astronomer very wealthy eccentric astronomer had managed to convince the Danish government to fund this extremely expensive observatory this in fact an entire island.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: Where he had taken decades of observations of all the planets Mars Jupiter every night at least every night for which the weather was clear with the naked eye actually this this was he was last of the of the naked eye astronomers. And so he had all this data which Kepler could use to confirm his theory.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: And so Kepler started working with with Tycho but Tycho was very jealous of the data he only gave him little bits of it at a time. And I think Kepler eventually just stole the data he he copied it and and had to have a fight with with Brahe's descendants.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: But he did work out he did get the data and then he worked out to kind of his disappointment that his beautiful theory didn't quite work like the data was sort of off from his Platonic solid theory by you know about 10% or something. And he found all kinds of fudges moving the circles around and...

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: things that it didn't quite work. But he worked on this problem for years and years and eventually he figured out how to use the data to work out the actual orbits of the planets and that was an incredibly clever genius amount of data analysis actually. And yeah, then he eventually worked out that the orbits were actually ellipses, not circles, which was shocking to him. And then he worked out the two laws of planetary motion: ellipses and also equal areas swept out in equal times.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: And then 10 years later, after collecting a lot of data, the furthest planets like Saturn and Jupiter were the hardest for him to work out, but then he finally worked out this third law also that the orbits, the time it takes for a planet to complete its orbit was proportional to some power of the distance to the sun. And these are the three famous Kepler's laws of motion, and he had no explanation for them. It was just all driven by experiment and it took Newton a century later to give a theory that explained all three laws at once.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: The take I want to try on you is that Kepler was a high temperature LLM. Where Newton comes up with this explanation of why the three laws of planetary motion must be true, and of course the way that Kepler discovers the laws of planetary motion or figures out the relative orbits of the different planets is, as you say, a work of genius. But then through his career, he's just trying random relationships.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And in fact, in the book in which he writes down the third law of planetary motion, it's sort of an aside on The Harmonics of the World, which is his book about, you know, all these different planets have these different harmonies and the reason there's so much famine and misery on earth is because the earth is mi-fa-mi, that's the note of earth. And so all this random astrology, but in there is the cube-square law, which tells you what relationship the period has to a planet's distance from the sun, which is, as you were detailing, if you add that to Newton's F equals MA and then the equation for centripetal acceleration, you get the inverse square law.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And so Newton works that out, but the reason I think this is an interesting story is I feel like LLMs could do the kind of thing of like 20 years, let's try random relationships, some of which make no sense, as long as there's a verifiable data bank like Brahe's data set where, okay, I'm going to try out random things about like musical notes. I'm going to try out random things about Platonic objects, all these different geometries. I have this bias that there's some important thing about the geometry of these orbits. And then one thing works and as long as you can verify it, it can then draw these empirical regularities, can then drive actual deep scientific progress.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: Traditionally when we talk about the history of science, idea generation has always been kind of the prestige part of science. So I mean, a scientific problem comes with many steps, you know, you have to identify a problem, and then you have to identify a good problem to work on, a fruitful problem. And then you need to...

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: to collect data, um you need to figure out a strategy to analyze the data, to make a hypothesis, um and at this point you need to propose a good hypothesis and then you need to validate it. Yeah so this and then you need to write things up and explain. There's there's a dozen different components. But yeah, the ones we celebrate are these sort of eureka genius moments of of idea generation. And um yeah, so Kepler certainly had to to as you say cycle through many ideas and several which didn't work and and I bet many that he didn't even um publish at all because yeah they they just didn't fit. And that's an important part of the process, um trying all kinds of of random things and seeing if they work.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: But as you say, the um you know the it it had to be matched by an equal amount of verification otherwise it's it's fluff. I mean um we celebrate Kepler but we should also celebrate Brahe for for his his assiduous data collection which was ten times more precise than than any previous observation.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: And it was um that extra decimal point of accuracy was actually essential for for Kepler to get um his um his results. Um and you know he was using you know Euclidean geometry and and like like the most advanced mathematics he could um use at the time to to match his his model with the data. So like all aspects had to be in play, you know the the data and the theory and the and the hypothesis generation. I'm not sure nowadays that hypothesis generation is the bottleneck anymore. Um science has changed in the in the century since. So um classically sort of the two big paradigms for for science were theory and experiment. Um then in the 20th century um numerical simulation came along and so you can also do do computer simulations of of to test theories. But then finally in late 20th century we had big data, you know we had the the era of data analysis. Um and so a lot of new progress is actually driven now by analyzing massive data sets first, collecting large data sets and then drawing the patterns from them to to deduce thoughts which is a little bit different from how science used to work where you make a few observations or you just have one out-of-the-blue idea and then you collect data to test your idea, that's the classic scientific method. Um now it's almost reversed, you collect big data first and then you you try to to get hypotheses from it. Um I mean Kepler was maybe one of the first early data scientists but but even he didn't start with Tycho's data set and and analyze it, he had some preconceived theories first. But it's it seems that this is less and less the way we make progress in um in science just because um yeah the data is is just so much more massive it's just so much more useful.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Oh interesting, I actually feel like the more the 20th century science that you're describing is actually very well describes what happened in Kepler where he did have these ideas in 1595 and

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: 96 is where he comes up with first polygons and then platonic objects theory, but they were wrong. And then a few years later he gets Brahe's data. And it's only after 20 years of just trying random things that he gets this empirical regularity. And so it actually feels closer to Brahe's data is analogous to some massive data bank of simulations, and then now he - now that you've got the data, you can keep trying random things. But if it wasn't, Kepler would be out there just writing books about harmonics and platonic objects, and there would be nothing to actually verify against.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah, yeah. So the the data was extremely important, but the distinction I was trying to make was that sort of traditionally you make a hypothesis and then you test it against data. But now with machine learning and data analysis and statistics and so on, you can - you can start with data and through say statistics work out laws that were not perceived before.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: And he - so Kepler's - so Kepler's third law is a little bit like this. Except that for the third law, instead of having the thousand data points that Brahe had, Kepler had like six data points. Like every planet - you knew the length of the orbit and the distance to the sun, and there was like five or six data points, and he did what we would now call regression. You know, he could fit a curve to these six data points and he got a square-cube law, which was amazing. But actually he was quite lucky. I mean, that these six data points gave him the right conclusion.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: You know, it's - that's not enough data to be really reliable. There was a later astronomer, Johannes Bode, who took the same - the same data actually, the - the distances to the planets, and inspired by Kepler I think, he had a prediction that the - the distances to the planets formed basically a shifted geometric progression. That he also fit a curve. Except that there was one - there was one point missing. So there was a big gap between Mars and Jupiter. And his law predicted that there was a missing planet. So it was a kind of a crank theory, except when Uranus was discovered by Herschel, the - the distance to Uranus fit exactly this - this pattern. And then Ceres was discovered, this asteroid between - I think in the asteroid belt, and it also fit the pattern. So people got really excited that - that Bode had discovered this - this amazing new law of nature. But then Neptune was discovered and it was completely like way off. And, you know, and it - basically it was just a numerical fluke, you know, there was - there was six data points.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Interesting.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so maybe one reason why Kepler didn't highlight his third law as much as the first two laws is that maybe instinctively, even though he didn't have modern statistics, he kind of knew that with six data points, he had to be somewhat tentative with the - with the conclusion.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: I see. But maybe to ask the question about the analogy more explicitly, does this analogy make sense to if we have, you know, in future we'll have smarter and smarter AIs and we'll have millions of them, and then they can go out and hunt for all these empirical regularities?

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: It sounds like you don't think the bottleneck in science is finding more things that are for each given field the equivalent of the third law of planetary motion, so that then later on somebody can say, oh, we need a way to explain this, let's work out the math, here's the inverse square law of gravity.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: Right, so I think AI has basically driven the cost of idea generation down to almost zero, in a very similar way to how the internet drove the cost of communication down to almost zero. Which is an amazing thing, but it doesn't create abundance by itself. Yeah, so now the bottleneck is different. So we're now in a situation where suddenly people can generate thousands of theories for a given scientific problem, and now we have to verify them, evaluate them. And this is something which we have to change our structures of science to actually sort this out. So, you know, in fact traditionally we build walls, you know, so in the past, before we had AI slop, you know, we had amateur scientists, you know, create their own theories of the universe, many of which were basically of very little value. And so we've built these peer review publication systems and things to kind of filter out and try to isolate the high signal ideas to test. But now that we can generate these possible explanations at massive scale, and some of them are good and a lot are terrible, I mean, human reviewers, we're just, they're already being overwhelmed actually. Many journals are reporting AI-generated submissions are just flooding their submissions. So it's great that we can generate all kinds of things now with AI, but it means that the rest of the aspects of science have to catch up. So verification, validation, and assessing what ideas actually move the subject forward and which ones are dead ends or red herrings. And that's not something we know how to do at scale. For each individual paper, we can discuss it, have a debate among scientists and get to a consensus in a few years. But when we're generating, you know, a thousand of these every day, this doesn't work.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah. So I think there's this incredibly interesting question of if we have billions of AI scientists, not only how do you gauge which ones are real progress, but how do you, I mean, this is actually a question that human science has had to face and we've solved somehow. And I'm not sure how we solve this, but in any given field, let's say it's 1940s and there's, if you're at Bell Labs or if you're just generally, these new technologies coming out, pulse code modulation, basically how do you transfer signals, how do you digitize signals, how do you transmit them over analog wires, and then, but there's like all these papers about the engineering constraints there and the details, and then there's one which is like, comes up with the idea of the bit, which has implications across many different fields. And you need some system which can then look at that and say, okay, we need to apply this to probability, we need to apply this to computer science, etc.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: for in the future the AIs are coming up with you know the next version of this kind of unifying concept and how would you identify it among millions of papers which might actually constitute progress but which have much less general unifying ideas

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: Right. So a lot of it is the test of time. Um, so so many great ideas didn't actually get a great reception at the time that they were first proposed. It was only after some other scientists realized that that they could take them further and apply them to their own um, you know deep learning itself actually was a niche area of AI for a long time. That this the idea of of getting answers entirely through training on data and and not through first principles you know reasoning was was was very controversial and then it would just took a long time before it actually started bearing fruit. You know, you mentioned the bit, you know, I mean there are there are other proposals for computer architectures than the 0-1 that is universal today. I think there there were trits you know 0-1-2 yes a three-valued logic and you know in an alternate universe maybe a different paradigm would have would have showed up.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: People have argued that you know the transformer for example is is the foundation of all modern large language models. And it was the first um deep learning architecture that really was was sophisticated enough to capture language. But it didn't have to be that way. There there could have been some other architecture that um was the first to do it and and once that was adopted um it would just become the standard.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: Um, so I think uh one reason why it's hard to assess whether a given idea is going to be fruitful is that it it depends on the future. It it depends on and it it depends on on also on the culture and society like like which ones get adopted, which ones don't. Um, you know the base 10 uh numeral system in mathematics extremely useful. Uh much better than the Roman numeral system for instance. Uh but again there's nothing special about 10. Um it's it's a system that we it's useful for us because everyone else uses it. Um and we've standardized it and we've brought all our uh computers and our and our number representation systems around it um and so we're stuck with it now actually. Um you know people occasionally push for other systems than decimal but um there's this no there's too much inertia.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: So you can't look at any given scientific achievement purely in isolation and give it an objective grade uh without being aware of the context both in the past and the future. And so it it may never be something that you can just reinforcement learn the same way that that you can for much sort of more localized problems.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: It seems often in the history of science when what when a new theory comes up that in retrospect we realize is correct, it seems to make implications that just either make no sense because they're wrong and we realize later on why they're wrong, or they're correct but seem wildly implausible at the time. So in as you talked about Aristarkus uh had heliocentrism in the third century BC and then the ancient Athenians were like this can't...

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: B, because it would be if the Earth is going around the sun, we should see the relative position of the stars change as we're going around the sun. And the only way that wouldn't be the case is if they're so far away that you don't notice any parallax, which is actually the correct implication. But there's times when actually the implication is incorrect and we just need to graduate to a better level of understanding. So Leibniz would, you know, chide Newton and disagree with Newton's theory of gravity on the basis that it implied action at a distance, and then there's we don't know the mechanism, and Newton himself was sort of stunned that inertial mass and gravitational mass were the same quantity. So all these things which were resolved by Einstein. But it was still progress. And so the question for a system of pure AGI would be, even if you can falsify a theory, how would you notice that it still constitutes progress relative to the thing before?

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: Yes, yes. So it often actually the ultimately correct theory initially is worse in many ways. Yeah. So Copernicus' theory of the planets, it was less accurate than Ptolemy's theory. You know, so geocentrism had been developed for a millennium by that point and they had made many, many tweaks and increasingly complex ad hoc fixes to make it more and more accurate. And Copernicus' theory was a lot simpler, but much less accurate. It was only Kepler that made it more accurate than Ptolemy's theory. I mean, science is always a work in progress. You know, so when you only get part of the solution, it looks worse than a theory which is incorrect, but somehow it has been completed to the point where it kind of answers all the questions. As you say, Newton's theory had big mysteries, the equivalence of mass and action at a distance, which were only resolved with a very conceptually different approach centuries afterwards. Often progress has been made actually not by adding more theories, but by deleting some assumptions that you have in your mind. So one reason why geocentrism held on for so long is we had this idea that objects naturally want to stay at rest. This is the Aristotelian notion of physics. And so the idea that the Earth was moving, how come we weren't all sort of all falling over? Once you have Newton's laws of motion, object in motion remains in motion and so forth, then it makes sense, but you had to sort of conceptually... it's a very big conceptual leap to realize that the Earth is in motion.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: It's in motion, it doesn't feel like it's in motion. And like the biggest advances, you know, Darwin's theory of evolution, the idea that species are not static, but it's not obvious because you don't see evolution in your lifetime.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: Well, now we can actually, but...

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: ...but it seems permanent and static.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: You know, right now we're going through an...

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: And a cognitive version of the Copernican revolution where we used to think that human intelligence is the center of the universe. And now we're I guess seeing that there's there's very different types of intelligence that that are out there with very different strengths and weaknesses. Um and so our assessment of which tasks require intelligence which ones don't, um has to be reordered quite a bit. Um and so yeah, it's it's trying to fit AI into sort of our theories of scientific progress and and and what is hard and what is easy. We're struggling quite a bit, but we have to ask questions that we've never really had to ask before. Or maybe the philosophers had, but now we all have to deal with it.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: This actually brings up a topic I've been very curious about. So you mentioned Darwin's theory of evolution. There's this book 'The Clockwork Universe' by Edward Dolnick which covers a lot of this era of history we're talking about. And he has this interesting observation in there that uh the 'Origin of Species' is published in 1859, the 'Principia Mathematica' is published in 1687. So the 'Origin of Species' comes out basically two centuries after the 'Principia'. And conceptually it seems like Darwin's theory is simpler. Um there's a contemporaneous biologist to Darwin who reads 'The Origin of Species', Thomas Huxley, and he says, 'How stupid not to have thought of that.' And nobody ever says that about 'Principia', the chiding themselves for not having beaten Newton to gravity. And so there's a question of, well, why did it take longer? It seems like a big part of the reason is that the evidence for natural selection is cumulative and retrospective, whereas Newton can just like, here's my equations, let me see the moon's orbital period and its distance, and if it lines up, then we've made progress. And so Lucretius actually had the idea, this idea that species adapted to their environment in the first century BC, but nobody ever like really talks about it until Darwin because Lucretius can't run some experiment and people are like forced to pay attention. And so I wonder if we'll in retrospect end up seeing much more progress in domains which are have this kind of tight data loop where you can verify them quite easily even though they were conceptually much more difficult.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: I think one one aspect of science is it's it's not just um creating a new theory and validating it, um but communicating it to others. Um so Darwin was actually an amazing science communicator. Um he wrote in English in natural— oh natural language I'm speaking like a—

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah, okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: Okay, my my yeah okay um I have to I have to sort of get out of my my technical mindset. Yeah okay so he spoke in plain English, um you know didn't use equations, and he synthesized a lot of um disparate facts yeah so you know little pieces of evolution had been worked out in the past but he had this very compelling vision. And um again still missing things like he he didn't know the mechanism for for um heredity um he didn't know DNA, okay and um yeah but uh his writing style was persuasive and that that helped a lot. Um Newton wrote in Latin, um he had had to invent you know entire new—

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: He used mathematics just to explain what he was doing. Um, he was also from an era which was a, where scientists were much more secretive and competitive. Um, so you know, academia is still competitive but it was even worse back in Newton's day. Um, so he, he held back some of his best insights because he didn't want his rivals to get any advantage. Um, he was also, okay, somewhat unpleasant person from what I, what I, what I gather, actually. Um, so, um, it was actually only a couple decades after Newton where other scientists explained his work in much simpler terms that they became, um, widespread.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: Um, so, um, yeah, the, the art of exposition and making the case, and, and creating a narrative, um, is, um, is also a very important part of science. Um, and, um, if you have the data and it helps, but, but people need to be convinced. Otherwise they will not push it further. Or they will not take the initial investment to, to learn your theory and really, and really explore it. Um, and that's another thing which is really hard to reinforcement learn on. Uh, how can you score how persuasive you are? Okay, well, okay, there's entire marketing departments who are trying to do this. So maybe it's good that AI are not yet optimized to be persuasive.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: So yeah, there's a social aspect to science. Um, the, um, you, you know, even though we pride ourselves on having an objective, um, side to it where there's data and there's experiment and validation, um, we still have to tell stories and convince our fellow scientists. Um, and that's a soft squishy thing. Like it's, you know, it's a combination of data and, um, yeah, and, um, painting a narrative. And it's a narrative of gaps, you know, I mean, as, you know, so even Darwin, as he said, there were, there were pieces of his theory he could not explain. But he could still make a case that, you know, in the future people would, um, would, would find transitional forms, they would find the mechanism for inheritance, and they did. Um, yeah, um, I don't know how you can quantify that in such a precise way that you can start to reinforcement learning. Um, maybe that will still be forever the human side of science.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Okay, speaking of clever ideas, one of my listeners, Sean, solved the puzzle that Jane Street made for my audience and posted a great walkthrough on X. For context, Jane Street trained a ResNet, and then shuffled all 96 layers, and then challenged people to put them back in the right order using only the model's outputs and training data. You can't brute force this. There's more possible orderings than atoms in the universe. So Sean broke the problem into two different parts. First, pair the layers into 48 different blocks, and second, put those blocks in the right order. For pairing, Sean realized that in a well-trained ResNet, the product of two weight matrices in a residual block should have a distinctive negative diagonal pattern. And this arises as a way for the model to keep the residual stream from growing out of control. From this insight, he was able to recover the right pairings. For ordering, Sean noticed that the model seemed to improve if he sorted the blocks by the size of their residual contributions. Starting with that rough approximation, he combined a clever ranking heuristic with local swaps to recover the exact right order. His full walkthrough is linked in the description. Don't worry if you didn't get to this puzzle in time though. There's still one up about backdoor LLMs that even Jane Street doesn't know how to solve. You can find it at janestreet.com/dwarkesh. All right, back to Terence.

And so ideally the author will talk — talk their way through, you know, what's important, what's not, but sometimes they don't reveal what what steps were the important ones and which ones are just kind of boilerplate standard uh steps. But you can study each lemma in isolation and some of them I can see...

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: Oh, this looks very standard. This resembles something I'm familiar with. I'm pretty sure there's nothing interesting going on here. But this lemma, oh, that's something I haven't seen before. And I could see why if you had this result, that would really help prove the main result. You can assess whether something's key to your argument or not. And Lean really facilitates that. The individual steps are identified really precisely. I think in the future, there'll be entire professions of mathematicians who might take a giant Lean-generated proof and maybe do some ablation on it or try to remove steps of parts of it and try to find more elegant ways, maybe some other AIs to do some reinforcement learning, how can you make the proof more elegant, and maybe other AIs would grade whether this proof looks better or not. full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: So it is... it's not clear... If there was some similar framework... so the bottleneck for using AI to create strategies and make conjectures is we have to rely on human experts and the test of time to validate whether something's plausible or not.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: If there was some semi-formal framework where this could be done semi-automatically in a way that isn't sort of easily hackable to... you know, if... so of course it's really important with these formal proof assistants that there are just no backdoors or exploits that you can do to somehow get your certified proof without actually proving it, because reinforcement learning is just so, so good at finding these backdoors. But, yeah, if some framework that sort of mimics how scientists talk to each other in a semi-formal way, of using data and argument, but also constructing narratives and... there's some subjective aspect of science that we don't know how to capture in a way that we can insert AI into them in any useful way.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Interesting.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: So yeah, this is a future problem. I mean, there are research efforts to, you know, to try to create automated conjecturers and maybe there are ways to benchmark these and get some way to simulate this, but this is all very, very new science.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Can you help me get some intuition for... I have two sub-questions. One, it would be very helpful to have a tangible sense of... it would be helpful to have a specific example of what something like this would look like, the way scientists communicate that we can't formalize yet. And two, it seems almost...

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: traditionally paradoxical to say he building up some narrative or building up some natural language explanation and then also having something which you could have formalized and I'm sure there's some intuition behind where that overlap is and I'd love to understand that better.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: Right, so, so an example of of a conjecture. So, Gauss was interested in the prime numbers and he computed he created one of the first mathematical data sets. He just computed the first 100,000 prime numbers or so, hoping to find patterns.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: And he did find a pattern, but maybe not not the pattern he was expecting. He he found a statistical pattern in the primes, that if you count how many primes there are up to 100, 1,000, 1 million and so forth, they get sparser and sparser but the the the drop-off in in the density was inversely proportional to the natural logarithm of of of the range of numbers.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: So he conjectured what we now call the prime number theorem. The number of primes up to x is like x divided by the natural log of x.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: And he had no way to prove this. Um, it was it was data-driven. Um, so this this was a conjecture. It was revolutionary for its time because um it was maybe the first really important conjecture of math that was statistical in nature, you know?

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: So normally you'd talk about patterns like maybe the spacing between the primes has a certain regularity or something, but uh yeah, but this was really something which it didn't tell you exactly how many primes there were in any given range. It just gave you an approximate approximation that got better and better as you went further and further out.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: But it uh it helped or so it it started the field of what we call analytic number theory. Um, but it was the first in many conjectures like this, many of which got proved, which sort of started um consolidating the idea that the prime numbers actually didn't really have a pattern, that they behaved like random um random sets of numbers with a certain density.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: I mean, they had some patterns like like they're almost all odd, okay, so there's some... and they're not actually random. They're what's called pseudo-random. I mean, um there's no random number generation involved in creating the prime numbers. But over time it became more and more productive to think of the primes as as if they were just generated by some some some god rolling dice all the time and just creating this this random set.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: And this allowed us to make all these other predictions. Um, so there's this still open conjecture in in number theory called the the twin prime conjecture, that there should be infinitely many pairs of primes that are twins, distance two apart, like 11 and 13. We can't prove that, and there's actually good reasons why we can't prove that, but um uh but because of this statistical random model of the primes, we are absolutely convinced it's true.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: Because we know that if the primes were sort of generated by flipping coins or something, that we would just by random chance, just like infinite monkeys at a typewriter, we would see um twin primes appear over and over again.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: conceptual model of what the primes should behave like based on statistics and probability, um but it's all mostly heuristic and non-rigorous, um but extremely accurate. Um so the few times when we actually can prove things about the primes, it has matched up with the predictions of this what we call the random model of of the primes. So we we have this conjectural framework for understanding the primes that we everyone believes in and you know it's the same reason why we believe the Riemann hypothesis is true, why we believe that cryptography based on the primes is basically um is mathematically secure and things like that. It's it's all part of this this this belief. Um in fact one reason why we care about the Riemann hypothesis is that if the Riemann hypothesis failed, um we knew it was false, it means that there would be it would be a serious blow to this model that that this it would mean there's a secret pattern to the primes that we were not aware of. Um and uh I think we would very rapidly abandon any cryptography based on the primes because if there was one pattern that we didn't know about there's probably more. And uh these patterns can lead to exploits in in crypto and yeah it's it's gonna be a big big shock. Um so we really want to make sure that that doesn't happen. Um so yeah it's it's um so we've been convinced of of things like the Riemann hypothesis and things over time but uh some of it is experimental evidence, some is uh the few times we've been able to make theoretical results they've always aligned. Um you know it is possible that that the consensus is wrong and and we've all just missed something very basic. Um you know there have been paradigm shifts in the past in fact history. Um yeah but we don't really have a way of measuring this. Um I think partly because we don't have enough data on on on how math or science develops. We have one timeline of history and you know we have like you know a hundred stories of turning points in history. If if if we had access to a million alien civilizations and each with a with a different development of history and of science in different order then maybe we'd actually have a have a have a decent shot at at at understanding of how do we measure what is uh progress and and what is a good strategy and we could maybe start formalizing it and and actually having a framework. Um maybe if um what we need to do is actually start creating lots of mini-universes or simulations of of AI solving very basic problems, you know in arithmetic or whatever but um but coming up with their own strategies for doing these things and and having these little laboratories to test. I mean there are people who who investigate like trying to find what's the smallest uh you know neural network that can do ten-digit multiplication and things like that. I think I think we could actually learn a lot just from evolving um small AIs on on on simple problems, we could learn a lot.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: You have to learn about new fields not only very rapidly, but deeply enough to contribute to the frontier. So in some sense, you're also one of the world's greatest autodidacts. What, how does, what is your process of learning about a new subfield in math? What does that look like?

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so I certainly identify with kind of the, yeah, as we talked about depth and breadth before. And it's not purely human-AI distinction. I mean, humans also split, so I think it was Isaiah Berlin who split them into hedgehogs and foxes. And the hedgehog knows one thing very, very well, and a fox knows a little bit about everything. So I definitely identify—you know, I think of myself as a fox.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: You know, I work with hedgehogs a lot, and sometimes I can be a hedgehog if need be, but—

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so I've always had a little bit of an obsessive streak. If there's something which I read about which I feel like I should understand—I have the capability to understand this, but I don't understand why it works, there's some magic in it that, you know, someone was able to use a type of mathematics I'm not familiar with and get a result which I would like to prove and I can't do it by myself, but they could do it by their method—then I want to find out what was their trick. It bugs me that someone else can do something which I think I can do but that I can't.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: So I've always had that kind of obsessive completionist-type streak. I've had to wean myself off computer games because I'll start a game and I want to play it to completion, all the levels. So that's one way in which I learn new fields. I collaborate with a lot of people who have taught me other types of mathematics. I just make friends with a mathematician who's working on another area of mathematics and I find their problems interesting, but they have to teach me some of the basic tricks and what's known and what's not known, and I learn a lot from that.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: I've found that writing about what I've learned via a blog, where I sometimes record things that I've learned, because in the past, when I was younger, I would learn something and it's called—

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: ...trick it is, okay. I'm going to remember this. And then six months later, I've forgotten. I remember remembering it, but I don't—I can't reconstruct my arguments. And the first few times it was so frustrating to have understood something and then lost it. And so I resolved I should always write down anything cool that I've learned. And that's—this is part of why how this blog came about.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: How long does it take you to write a blog post?

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: Um, it's something I often do when I don't want to do other work. You know, like, there's some referee report or something. There's—there's some thing that—that feels slightly unpleasant for me to do at the time. And so, writing a blog, it feels creative and fun, like it's—it's something that—that I do for myself. Um, so maybe, depending on the topic, it could be a quick, you know, half an hour or several hours. But I—um, it doesn't—because it's something that I do sort of voluntarily, it doesn't feel like it—it—it doesn't feel—time flies when I—when I write these things. As opposed to sort of doing something which I have to do for administrative reasons, but it's just—it's just drudgery. Okay. Those are tasks where AI is really helping with nowadays, actually.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Is it, um, if like civilization could from first principles decide how to use Terry Tao's time. You know, it's like a limited resource. How—how—what is the biggest diff between in the—if the veil of ignorance got to decide how to use Terry Tao's time versus what it does now?

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: This podcast wouldn't be happening.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: Yeah. So actually the—as much as I complain about certain tasks that I don't want to do but I have to do. So as—as you get more senior in—in academia, you get more and more responsibilities, you know, it's like more committees and—and whatever. Um, but I have also found that, um, a lot of events that I kind of reluctantly went to because I was obliged to for one reason or another, um, because it's outside my comfort zone, I often find interactions with people who I wouldn't normally talk to, like you for instance. And I would learn interesting things and—and have interesting experiences. Um, and I would have opportunities to—to then network with other people that I would never have—have done before. Um, so I do believe a lot in serendipity. Um, I mean, I do optimize my time in—in a—um, so there's some portions of—of my day where I do schedule very carefully. Um, but I have been willing to sort of leave some—some portions just okay, I'm going to do something which is—which is not my usual thing, and—and maybe it'll be a waste of my time, but maybe I'll—I'll learn something. And—and more often than not, it's—um, I've—I feel like I've—I've gotten a positive experience which is not something I would have planned for. Um, and yeah, so I believe a lot in serendipity. Um, and maybe there's a danger actually that, you know, in modern societies, it's not just AI, but we've become really good at optimizing everything. Um, and—and maybe we are optimizing—we're not optimizing our optimization. Um, that, you know, with—with...

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: COVID, for example, we switched, like, we switched a lot to remote meetings, and so everything was scheduled now. And so we kept busy, at least in academia, you know, we met almost the same number of people that we met in person, but everything had to be planned, you had to schedule things in advance. And what we lost out on was sort of the casual, like, knocking on a hallway, just meeting someone while getting coffee, and the this serendipitous interactions that you may think are not optimal, but actually are really important. You know, when I was a grad student, I would go down to the library to look for a journal article. You had to physically go down to the library, check out the journal, and read your article. And sometimes the next article, you know, you can just browse through and then the next article was also interesting. Sometimes it wasn't, but you could accidentally find interesting things, which is something which has basically been lost now because you can just type in what you want, if you want to access an article now, you just type it into a search engine or even an AI and you can get instantly what you want, but you don't get sort of the accidental things that you might have had if you'd done it more inefficiently. So, yeah. There've been times when I spent a year once at the Institute for Advanced Study, which is a great place to, you know, there's no distractions, you're there to just do research, and like the first few weeks you're there, like it's great, you're getting all these papers written up that you've been wanting to do for a long time, you've been thinking about problems for blocks of hours of time. But I find if I stay there for more than several months, like I run out of inspiration somehow, like I get bored, I actually surf the internet a lot more. You actually do need a certain level of distraction in your life, it somehow adds enough randomness and high temperature, high temperature you need. So, yeah, I don't know the optimal way to schedule my life. It just seems to work.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: I'm very curious when you expect AI to like actually do frontier math better than the at least as good as well as the best human mathematician?

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: I mean, in some ways they're already doing frontier math that is super-intelligent that humans can't do, but it's a different frontier from what we're used to. I mean, you could argue that calculators were doing frontier math that humans could not accomplish, but it wasn't, you know, number crunching, but...

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: But replacing Terry Tao completely?

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: I mean, which is what do you want me for? I...

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: You'll just go on other podcasts after.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: I'm not sure we... it might not be the right question to ask.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: I think within a decade.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: A lot of things that mathematicians currently do, we spend a lot of the bulk of our time doing it and a lot of the stuff we put in our papers today can be done by AI. But we'll find that that actually wasn't the most important part of what we do.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: You know, 100 years ago, a lot of mathematicians were just solving differential equations. People needed, physicists needed some exact solution to some system and they just hired a mathematician to laboriously go through the calculus and work out the solution to this fluid equation or whatever. A lot of what a 19th-century mathematician would do, you could make a call to Mathematica or Wolfram Alpha or a computer algebra package or now more recently an AI, and it would just solve the problem in a few minutes. But we moved on, we worked on different types of problems after that. You know, once computers came along, computers used to be human, right? People used to be laboriously creating log tables and work out primes as Gauss did, and that has all been outsourced to computers. But we moved on. In genetics, you know, to sequence the genome of a single organism, that was an entire PhD of a geneticist, you know, slowly separating all the chromosomes and whatever. And now you can just spend $1,000 and send it to a sequencer and get it done. But genetics is not dead as a subject. You move to a different scale, you know, maybe you study whole ecosystems rather than individuals.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: I take your point, but on the question of well, when is most mathematical progress and almost all mathematical progress happening by AI? So if you find out oh this theorem of Millennium Prize problem has been solved, you would put you know a 95% odds that an AI did it autonomously. Surely there will be such a year.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: I guess, I mean, I do believe that hybrid human plus AIs will dominate mathematics for a lot longer. It will require some additional breakthroughs beyond what we already have, so it's going to be stochastic. I think, you know, AI is currently very good at certain things but really terrible at others. And while you can sort of add more and more frameworks on top to kind of reduce the error rates and make them work with each other a bit more and so forth, I feel like we don't have all the ingredients to like really have a truly satisfactory sort of replacement for all intellectual tasks. It is complementary currently, it is not a replacement. But maybe, I mean, because current level of AIs will accelerate science in so many ways, hopefully, you know, new discoveries, new breakthroughs will happen more quickly.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: it's possible that also by somehow just through serendipity we we actually inhibit certain types of progress. Anything is possible really at this point. I think this- the world is very, very unpredictable at this point in time.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: What is your advice to somebody who would consider a career in math or is early in a career in math, especially in light of AI progress? How should they be thinking about their career differently, if at all, as a result of AI progress?

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so, uh, we live in a time of change. Um, it is, as I said, like it- we live in a particularly unpredictable era. Um, and, uh, I think in terms of, like, things that we've taken for granted for centuries may not hold anymore. Um, so, um, yeah, the way we, uh, do everything, not just mathematics, will change. And, um, you know, so I think, uh, which is, you know, I mean, in many ways, I would prefer the much more boring, quiet era where things are much the same as they were 10 years ago or 20 years ago. But, uh, so I think one just has to embrace this, that this- there's going to be a lot of change. Um, and that, uh, you know, the things that you study, some of them may become obsolete or revolutionized. But, but some things will be retained. Um, and, uh, um, so you somehow always have to keep an eye on the... yeah, there'll be a lot of opportunities for things that you wouldn't be able to do before. Um, so I mean, in math, you previously had to basically go through years and years of education, be a math PhD before you could contribute to the frontier of, of math research. Um, but now it's quite possible at a high school level or whatever that you could get involved in a math project and actually make a real contribution because of all these AI tools and, and Lean and everything else. Um, so there'll be a lot of non-traditional opportunities to learn. Um, so you need a very adaptable, um, mindset. Um, you know, there'll be room for pursuing things just for curiosity, for playing- playing around and, and, uh, I mean, you still need to get your credentials for- I mean, for a while, it'll still be important to sort of still go through traditional education and, and learn math and science and so forth the old-fashioned way for a while. But, um, yeah, uh, but you should also be open to, to very, very different ways of, of doing science, some of which don't exist yet. Uh, yeah, so it's- it's a scary time but also very exciting. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Awesome. That's a great note to close on. Terence, thanks so much.

full_transcript.speaker: Terence Tao
full_transcript.text: Yeah, thanks. Pleasure.

highlights: AI has driven the cost of idea generation toward zero, shifting the primary bottleneck of science from coming up with hypotheses to verifying and validating them at scale.

Current AI tools excel at mathematical "breadth," allowing them to solve numerous neglected problems by applying standard techniques, whereas human experts still provide the necessary "depth" for solving historically resistant core problems.
Scientific progress often depends on narrative and persuasion; even when data is correct, a theory must be communicated effectively to the scientific community to be adopted and built upon.

The formalization of mathematics through tools like Lean allows for a future where complex AI-generated proofs can be systematically refactored or "ablated" by humans to extract and understand the underlying conceptual insights.
Over-optimizing research and schedules through digital tools risks losing "serendipity," the accidental but essential breakthroughs that occur during unplanned interactions and inefficient browsing.
summary:
The conversation begins with an analysis of Johannes Kepler’s discovery of planetary motion, illustrating a fundamental shift in the scientific method from theory-driven models to data-driven analysis. Tao and Patel observe that while Kepler initially sought a "beautiful" geometric theory, it was Tycho Brahe’s rigorous data that forced him toward the discovery of elliptical orbits. This serves as a metaphor for the modern AI era: Tao argues that AI has driven the cost of idea generation toward zero, shifting the scientific bottleneck from the "eureka" moment of hypothesis generation to the arduous task of verification and validation. As AI floods fields with high-volume suggestions, the challenge lies in distinguishing "signal" from "slop" and developing new systems to evaluate progress at scale.

In the context of mathematics, Tao discusses the recent success of AI in solving dozens of Erdős problems, noting that these tools currently excel at "breadth"—the ability to apply standard techniques to a vast array of problems that humans have overlooked. However, he distinguishes this "artificial cleverness" from true intelligence, noting that current models struggle with "depth," or the ability to build cumulative, adaptive strategies over time. To move beyond this plateau, Tao emphasizes the importance of formal proof assistants like Lean, which allow for the precise deconstruction of complex arguments. He envisions a future where we develop a formal language not just for proofs, but for mathematical *strategies* and heuristics, allowing humans and machines to collaborate on a conceptual level rather than just a technical one.

Ultimately, Tao views AI as a "trustworthy co-author" that is already revolutionizing the auxiliary tasks of research, such as coding, data visualization, and literature searches. He compares the coming shift in mathematics to the automation of log tables or genome sequencing; while the "bulk" work of 19th-century mathematicians is now handled by machines, the discipline itself will evolve toward higher-level conceptual challenges. Tao describes his own approach to research as a "fox"—favoring breadth and serendipitous interactions—and advises aspiring scientists to remain adaptable. He concludes that while the core of human mathematical intuition remains distinct for now, the traditional path to the mathematical frontier is being permanently reshaped by AI’s ability to map out the "low-hanging fruit" of the intellectual landscape.
description:
We begin the episode with the absolutely ingenious and surprising way in which Kepler discovered the laws of planetary motion.

People sometimes say that AI will make especially fast progress at scientific discovery because of tight verification loops. But the story of how we discovered the shape of our solar system shows how the verification loop for correct ideas can be decades (or even millennia) long.

During this time, what we know today as the better theory can often actually make worse predictions (Copernicus's model of circular orbits around the sun was actually less accurate than Ptolemy's geocentric model). And the reasons it survives this epistemic hell is some mixture of judgment and heuristics that we don’t even understand well enough to actually articulate, much less codify into an RL loop.

Hope you enjoy!
